title: Roberto Franzosi: From Words to Numbers: Narrative, Data, and Social Science.
abstract: From Words to Numbers by Roberto Franzosi gives conflicting messages. First, Franzosi claims that he has techniques that allow us to get rid of a lot of words and replace them with fewer words and numbers. Second, he is a florid sociological gossip. He does not lead by example. Does an analytic heart beat under his ornate (but enjoyable) prose? Yes, but I do not think that its significance is as great as Franzosi claims. The central contribution of this book is Franzosi's description of his story grammar approach to coding texts, which he developed to overcome what he perceived to be the shortcomings of content analysis. He deems content analysis inadequate because the coded output is contaminated by the theoretical prejudices of the researcher. In content analyses, text is coded into categories that the researcher believes are pertinent. As a consequence, Franzosi argues, the output of content analyses has limited utility to scholars with different theoretical proclivities, the original input text is unrecoverable from the coding output and the output is highly vulnerable to coding errors by researchers (p. 60–1).
